_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 9-5 EMS Medication and IV Supply Requirements (Revised 4/28/2023, MDHHS Reviewed 2023). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
condition: EMS Medication and IV Supply Requirements
protocol_reference: Michigan Medication Protocol Section 9-5: EMS Medication and IV Supply Requirements — MDHHS Approved 4/28/2023
purpose: Govern EMS agency medication and IV supply stocking, sealing, controlled substance management, and discrepancy reporting. Medication boxes must be sealed and tamper-evident. Controlled substance requirements must comply with DEA regulations and Michigan law. Discrepancies must be reported promptly.

sections.title: Medication Box Sealing Requirements
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 9-5: EMS Medication Requirements — Sealing
points: All EMS medication/drug boxes must be sealed with tamper-evident seals when not in active use
Seal must include: seal serial number, date sealed, and name/signature of person sealing
Seal integrity must be verified at each shift change; broken or missing seal without documented explanation requires immediate supervisor notification and documentation
Medication box seal exchange must occur per MCA-approved pharmacy exchange protocol (see Section 9-6)

sections.title: Pharmacy Exchange Process
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 9-5: EMS Medication Requirements — Pharmacy Exchange
points: Depleted or used medication boxes must be exchanged with fresh sealed boxes via the MCA-approved pharmacy exchange program
Exchange must occur at an MCA-authorized exchange location (hospital, pharmacy, or MCA-designated site)
Used box must be sealed and labeled with the date, call number or PCR number, and signature of exchanging provider
Documentation of the exchange must be retained per MCA records requirements

sections.title: Controlled Substance Management
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 9-5: EMS Medication Requirements — Controlled Substances
points: Controlled substances must be stored in a locked, secure compartment separate from non-controlled medications when required by DEA regulations
Controlled substance administration must be documented with: drug name, dose administered, remaining quantity, time, and two-provider verification when required by MCA policy
Controlled substance waste: must be witnessed and documented per DEA and MCA requirements
All controlled substance discrepancies (shortages, overages, unaccounted doses) must be reported immediately to the agency supervisor and MCA per Section 9-5 reporting requirements

sections.title: Discrepancy Reporting
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 9-5: EMS Medication Requirements — Discrepancy Reporting
points: Any medication discrepancy (missing medication, incorrect quantity, tampered seal without documentation, or unaccounted controlled substance) must be reported to the agency supervisor immediately upon discovery
Agency must report controlled substance discrepancies to the MCA and, if required, to the DEA and MDHHS within required timeframes
Providers must not remove, use, or tamper with medications outside of authorized patient care circumstances
Discrepancy investigation findings must be documented and retained per MCA records requirements

deterioration_flags: Controlled substance discrepancy identified — report to supervisor immediately; do not delay reporting
scope_notes: Medication boxes: tamper-evident seal required; verify at shift change
Broken seal without documentation: notify supervisor immediately
Pharmacy exchange: at MCA-authorized location; document exchange
Controlled substances: locked storage; two-provider verification when required; waste witnessed and documented
Discrepancies: notify supervisor immediately; DEA/MDHHS notification per applicable requirements
